data.title: The London Company Income Equity Vs. Russell 1000 Value Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

U.S. equities were mostly lower in the first quarter of 2026. The Russell 3000 declined 4%, and the S&P 500 posted its first quarterly decline in a year. The year began on a constructive note as declining yields, improving leading indicators, and rising mortgage applications supported a broadening rally. However, sentiment reversed sharply in March following the Iran conflict escalation. The near-blockage of the Strait of Hormuz sent crude oil surging over 75%, reigniting inflation fears and shifting the Fed narrative from rate cuts to potential hikes.

Equity market leadership changed sharply as the quarter progressed. Early cyclical broadening gave way to a narrow, commodity-driven market that benefited energy, agriculture, and hard-asset industries. Large-cap Growth was the only style box to post double-digit losses, hampered by Big Tech weakness and AI displacement concerns in software. Conversely, Small Caps proved resilient; the Russell 2000 finished higher while the Equal-Weight S&P 500 remained flat. Sector dispersion was extreme in the S&P 500, with Energy surging over 35% while Technology fell over 9%. From a factor perspective, Value, Momentum, and Yield drove returns. Quality was mixed, while Growth and Volatility factors detracted.

Key Performance Takeaways

The London Company Income Equity portfolio returned 4.4% (4.2% net) during the quarter vs. a 4.2% decrease in the Russell 1000 Index. Both stock selection and sector exposure were tailwinds to relative performance.

The Income Equity portfolio produced positive absolute returns in a down quarter for the Russell 1000, exceeding our 75-80% downside capture expectations. Our portfolio thrived as the market broadened away from the Magnificent 7 and Large Cap Growth. Even with pockets of opportunity cost, as deep value and commodity-linked industries moved sharply higher, the strategy's combination of quality, high active share, dividends, and downside resilience proved to be well suited to an unsettled market. We were encouraged to see the portfolio play strong defense amidst the volatility with compelling participation as the market broadened.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

Corning Inc (GLW) – GLW continues to outperform, driven by strong demand in Optical Communications, particularly for GenAI-related products. This demand is supporting higher pricing and margins while accelerating progress toward its medium-term outlook. The scale of hyperscaler partnerships is also enhancing longer-term visibility. We believe GLW's diversified portfolio of innovative, value-added products is well positioned to capitalize on secular growth trends across its end markets.

Chevron Corporation (CVX) – CVX delivered exceptional returns as the price of crude increased to over $100 in response to escalating conflict in Iran. We do not speculate on the direction of commodity prices, and we generally do not favor commodity businesses given that the primary driver of results is outside their control. That said, we have a high degree of confidence in the management team at CVX. We appreciate the discipline and conservatism with which they run the business and the focus they place on shareholder returns.

Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (APD) – APD benefited from industrial gas demand improving and rising helium prices tied to the Iran conflict. Helium represents a low-teens percentage of earnings and has weighed on results in recent years due to a weak pricing environment. The current pricing tailwind should provide some near-term relief. Our long-term thesis continues to track against improved execution under new management.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

Nintendo Co., Ltd. ADR (NTDOY) – NTDOY was pressured this quarter by rising memory chip prices, a key input of its hardware, and concerns around increased competition in gaming from AI. Management does not expect memory prices to impact financials over the next year materially. Regarding the AI risk, we believe NTDOY's IP and integrated hardware-software ecosystem will become more valuable in a market saturated with low-cost content. We also see the upcoming new Mario Bros. movie as a potential catalyst for the stock and the Switch 2 ecosystem.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) – UNH detracted during the period after an initial government rate update for 2027 came in lower than expected, pressuring managed care stocks. This surprised investors and weighed on sentiment. However, further analysis suggested the initial rates were based on imperfect data, and final rates announced in April were meaningfully improved. We remain constructive on UNH given its scale, diversified earnings base, and disciplined underwriting, which position it to navigate rate cycles and support long-term earnings growth.

Paychex, Inc. (PAYX) – PAYX's multiple compressed this quarter due to concerns that AI-native software could increase competition and pressure pricing plus margins for incumbents in the human capital management market. However, the earnings report showed accelerating revenue growth, continued pricing power, and record-high retention rates. We believe trusted providers with access to human support will remain in demand for critical business functions such as payroll, tax, and benefits administration. PAYX trades at a 4.9% dividend yield, which is well covered by free cash flow and provides a cushion to an attractive earnings growth profile.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Overweight Industrials (a better performing sector) & underweight Information Technology (a weaker performing sector)
  • What Hurt: Overweight Financials (a weaker performing sector) & underweight Real Estate (a better performing sector)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Increased: Dominion Energy Inc (D) – Used additional cash to add to our D position. It continues to generate consistent, regulated earnings and should benefit from long-term demand growth from data center expansion. The dividend payout remains attractive and it is a defensive name.
  • Initiated: Restaurant Brands International Inc (QSR) – QSR is one of the world's largest restaurant franchisors operating Burger King, Tim Hortons, Popeyes, and Firehouse Subs. The company earns steady royalty and fee income from thousands of franchise locations worldwide, producing naturally high profit margins and strong, predictable cash flow. QSR trades at a meaningful discount to peers like McDonald's and Yum! Brands, largely due to concerns around Burger King's U.S. performance. However, management has laid out a clear plan to simplify the business and improve restaurant quality, targeting a nearly fully franchised model by 2028. We see this as a classic "self-help" story where the company's own actions drive improvement— not reliance on a strong economy. With durable global demand, growing international presence, an attractive dividend, and a clear path to closing the valuation gap with peers, we believe QSR is a strong long-term investment and fits well within our Quality-at-a-Reasonable-Price framework.
  • Increased: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B (BRK.B) – We added to BRK.B on relative weakness and the news around the resumption of its buyback program, signaling that the stock is trading under its intrinsic value. BRK.B offers defensive positioning with over $370B in cash, providing optionality in an uncertain macro environment. It maintains several quality characteristics with its capital allocation strategy and diversified earnings across insurance, rails, utilities, and other operating businesses.
  • Reduced: Corning Inc (GLW) – We trimmed GLW following the strength in shares, and the position had grown to an oversized weighting. The fundamentals remain intact around optical fiber demand and stable glass content growth. We still have conviction in the long-term thesis.

Looking Ahead

The situation in Iran and the broader macro backdrop remain very fluid. The coming months should provide greater clarity on whether recent events prove more inflationary, more demand-destructive, or some combination of both. Despite the many risks to the near-term outlook, we believe there are reasons for optimism as well. Economic data is generally positive with solid corporate earnings, rising GDP, as well as low unemployment. While we do not attempt to forecast the path of geopolitics or the broader economy, we recognize that macro-driven disruptions can overwhelm fundamentals in the short run. Even so, history suggests that supply shocks and geopolitical events, while meaningful in the near term, rarely redefine the long-term earnings power of advantaged businesses.

From an equity market perspective, breadth and leadership may remain uneven as markets digest shifting macro narratives. We continue to view the recent setback as a pause in a multi-year broadening cycle rather than a lasting reversal. Prior broadening episodes have often been choppy, but also durable, unfolding over years rather than quarters. In that environment, we believe portfolios emphasizing high-quality businesses with durable competitive advantages, strong returns on capital, pricing power, flexible balance sheets, and reasonable valuations are well positioned. In a market where breadth and leadership can shift quickly, we believe those characteristics position our portfolios to participate when fundamentals reassert themselves while helping to manage downside if volatility persists.

data.title: Sensient Technologies: The Bull Case Looks Priced In
data.description:
Thesis

It’s been almost three years since I last wrote about Sensient Technologies Corporation (SXT), a company behind the products you barely notice but absolutely experience: the colors in your drinks, the flavors in your snacks, the coatings on your pills, and the pigments in your cosmetics. Basically, Sensient sells the sensory layer.

Back then, I had a hold rating because I liked the company's long-term prospects; however, in the short term, there were several headwinds preventing me from being overly bullish toward SXT at that time, including destocking of inventory, volume weakness across multiple end user groups, and margin pressure on the company's profit. In my view, the potential for upside from the company did not outweigh my concern regarding these headwinds.

Initially, after the call, SXT had a rough go, trading down to some extent, but then it slowly built back and consolidated for a while. From there, almost a year ago, just before its Q1 2025 EPS and revenue beats, shares broke out significantly, eventually reaching all-time highs.

That said, after digging into Sensient’s latest Q1 2026 results and taking another look at my rating, I’m still staying neutral. I think that the natural color opportunity is clearly interesting. But, at the same time, the stock already seems to reflect a lot of that upside. And because expectations look pretty full, the margin for mistakes is extremely small.

Is This Growth Story Bigger Than It Looks?

  • Q1 2026 revenue: $435.83M
  • Revenue growth: 11.09% Y/Y
  • Local currency revenue growth: 7%
  • Color Group local currency revenue growth: 12.3%
  • Natural color conversion invoiced (last 9 months): ~$20M
  • Prior conversion invoiced (back half 2025): ~$5M

The revenue increase was across all product lines, with the Color Group being the standout; that unit has been accelerating because of both continued sales from the base business and other factors, such as the U.S. market gradually switching from synthetic to natural colors. This acceleration was two-pronged; growth in the base business continues to be achieved through product differentiation based on technical superiority. The Color Group’s growth cannot be attributed to volume pre-loads (product accumulated in customer inventory in advance of new orders), but rather to new wins added to the pipeline; new wins are driving most of the growth in the Color Group during the quarter. Pricing made a cameo, but it wasn’t exactly the main act. Most of the growth came the old-fashioned way: selling more stuff and selling better stuff.

  • Color Group adjusted EBITDA margin: 24.4%
  • Color Group local currency operating profit growth: 13.2%
  • Local currency adjusted EBITDA growth: 10%

Meanwhile, margins held up better than feared. Heading into the year, Sensient Technologies warned investors that profit margins in Color would probably dip because they were spending a ton of money building out capacity before the revenue from that capacity actually showed up. Instead, margins stayed flat. Why? Because the new customers they won were on the trickier, more complex end of color conversions, and those jobs pay better. This bodes well for future profit margins for Sensient Technologies. Management was clear: margins will trend flat for at least the first half of the year, and material improvement won’t become apparent until the fourth quarter of the calendar year. They are incurring expenses now and anticipating that revenue will ramp up shortly thereafter.

  • Natural color sales goal: $1B
  • Synthetic color revenue base: ~$100M
  • Conversion ratio: ~10:1

The fact that regulatory deadlines are real and companies have a vested interest in meeting them is beyond doubt. The current spike in natural colors is not just hype; there are hard deadlines forcing companies' hands. For example, Walmart (WMT) has committed to reformulating its private-label products by January 2027 to remove artificial food colors (FD&C). Synthetic food color products will no longer be permitted for sale in West Virginia beginning in January 2028. The state of Texas will require warning labels on all products containing artificial FD&C colorings as of January 2027. These changes represent shelf consequences. As such, customers are continuing to develop reformulated versions of their current products, despite worries about how the current economic climate may affect their ability to spend.

The process of switching from artificial to natural colors (for just one product) typically requires a time frame of six to twelve months. A supplier must not only substitute one ingredient for another, but also ensure the color remains stable, has the appropriate appearance, does not change the product’s existing flavor profile, and conforms to regulatory requirements. As suppliers, Sensient Technologies and some of its competitors, including GNT Group, Oterra, and DDW, assist their customers not only by selling the appropriate color but also by providing technical help and support throughout the reformulation process. Therefore, once a customer begins working with a manufacturer’s technical staff on a particular project, it becomes very time-consuming and financially expensive for that customer to switch to another supplier mid-process. My point in bringing this up is that this creates a true advantage for Sensient Technologies over its competitors. The worry: as the easier, more straightforward conversions get done, competition will get fiercer on the remaining harder ones. So it’s worth watching whether Sensient Technologies can hold onto that edge.

  • Flavors & Extracts local currency revenue growth: 1.7%
  • Flavors & Extracts local currency operating profit growth: 5.1%
  • Flavors & Extracts adjusted EBITDA margin: 17.2%
  • Asia Pacific local currency revenue growth: 4.7%
  • Asia Pacific local currency operating profit growth: 14.5%
  • Asia Pacific adjusted EBITDA margin: 26.1%

The Flavors and Asia Pacific divisions were also successful; each surpassed expectations, with profit levels growing at a faster rate than revenue levels in each division, demonstrating operational discipline. In Asia Pacific, management did caution that continued improvement will depend, in part, on the Iran conflict being resolved because of its impact on energy costs, shipping costs, and certain raw material costs. As you know, that’s completely out of their hands, and it’s a genuine wild card in this equation.

  • Q1 2026 cash flow used in operations: -$14M
  • Q1 2026 capex: $29M
  • 2026 capex guidance: $150M–$170M
  • Natural color capex plan (next few years): $225M–$250M
  • Net debt / credit adjusted EBITDA: 2.4x
  • Q1 2026 interest expense: $7.9M
  • Q1 2025 interest expense: $7.3M
  • Q2 2026 expected interest expense: ~$9M
  • 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance: high-single to double-digit growth
  • 2026 adjusted EPS guidance: high-single to double-digit growth

In the meantime, the company had negative cash flow this quarter, which was anticipated because it's a seasonal occurrence; moreover, Sensient Technologies has purposely built up inventory for the anticipated surge of natural color orders from its customers. Sensient's debt (as measured by a leverage ratio) is currently in the low twos; however, management believes the ratio will rise as the company continues to spend on factories and build up inventory. Management also feels comfortable with such an increase in debt because they still believe that the revenue will be forthcoming; even so, if their customers’ reformulation timelines slip, that financial cushion may diminish. Another point here is that management boosted their full-year profit (EBITDA) projection by a larger amount than they raised their EPS projection. If this may seem unusual, it's not. The reason is that, over the course of the year, borrowing costs (interest on all that debt) continue to increase and reduce the bottom line. One analyst from BNP Paribas caught this and flagged it; management confirmed it openly.

  • 2026 revenue guidance: high-single to double-digit local currency growth
  • Natural color sales goal: $1B
  • Natural color conversion invoiced (to date): ~$20M

Essentially, Sensient Technologies believes that the changeover from artificial to natural food colors will happen at a much more rapid pace than its current forecasts are predicting. As a result, they're building additional factories, sourcing necessary raw materials, and hiring employees accordingly. If they are correct, then they will look like geniuses for being so well prepared for the changeover process; however, if their customers take too long to put this strategy into effect, then they could have a stockpile of costly excess inventory and capacity. Right now, the early evidence suggests the bet is going reasonably well.

SXT's Valuation

As I write this, pre-market, it looks like SXT shares are getting an uppercut to the chin. It most likely has to do with the Iranian "wildcard" I just mentioned. Specifically, Brent Crude (Jun ’26) was trading at $126. That's not really good for most of us, nor SXT. As you know, this war has swung back and forth between tentative peace and renewed fighting. My read has been that the experts predicting a longer, potentially wider regional conflict have, so far, had the better odds than those of us who thought it might be resolved sooner.

Even before today's trading, on the raw numbers, SXT wasn’t cheap. At $120.54, FAST Graphs has it trading at a blended P/E of 33.33x, well above its normal 19.74x multiple and more than double the 15x fair value line. Meanwhile, that implied fair value comes in significantly lower, around $77.83, telling me that the market's already pricing in a substantial amount of success from the natural color conversion cycle.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s valuation table, it basically indicates the same thing in a different language: forward non-GAAP P/E is 31.33x versus the sector median of 16.05x, while forward EV/EBITDA is 17.63x versus 8.23x for the sector.

Part of the premium is justified by the growth profile. Forward revenue growth of 8.20%, forward EBITDA growth of 14.40%, and forward EPS growth of 16.47% are all above sector levels.

The longer-term EPS growth estimate of 17.50% also supports a higher multiple. My concern is that the stock price has risen faster than the business has turned profits into cash.

Cash flow is still negative, capex is rising, debt is likely to increase, and inventory is building ahead of customer conversions.

Final Takeaway

Hold. I’d stay patient here rather than chase it. SXT does have a real growth driver with the shift to natural colors. But, at this point, the stock already seems to be pricing in a good amount of that upside. It is also trading at a premium multiple, so the risk/reward no longer looks clearly attractive.

So, if I already owned it and believed conversions could pick up into 2027, I’d be fine holding. But for new money, I’d wait for a better entry point. And if I cared a lot about valuation, I definitely wouldn’t chase it here.

data.title: Varonis Systems: Potential For Topline Growth To Accelerate
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Varonis Systems (VRNS) a few years ago, which I recommended a buy rating as I expected the SaaS transition to drive growth acceleration, which should also help to drive a positive multiple re-rating, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. In my view, the transition has now played out well. The question is no longer whether VRNS can get through the model change. It mostly has. What matters now is whether the cleaner SaaS model, better new customer momentum, and broader AI-related demand can support the next leg of growth. I believe it can, and hence, I stay buy-rated.

Earnings review

VRNS started FY2026 strong. Total revenue was $173.1 million, up 27% y/y from $136.4 million in 1Q25. The mix kept moving toward SaaS. In the quarter, SaaS revenue was $161.1 million, up from $88.6 million vs. last year. Meanwhile, term license subscription revenue fell to $6.9 million from $31.5 million, while maintenance and services revenue fell to $5.2 million from $16.4 million. While adj. gross margin is down slightly from 80.2% last year to 77.9% this quarter, adj. EBIT margin actually went up, from -4.7% last year to -0.8%, which effectively means VRNS should turn profitable next year. The key takeaway here is that revenue growth accelerated, SaaS became a bigger share of the business, and non-GAAP profitability improved, even if gross margin compressed.

Post-transition growth is becoming more apparent

Fast forward almost 2.5 years since my last update, and the SaaS transition has clearly played out well. SaaS ARR excluding conversions grew 29% y/y to $522.6 million, which I think is the cleanest number in the quarter because it strips out the noise from customers moving from the old model to the new one. More importantly, the quarter also showed why the underlying business now looks better after the transition. Specifically, management said sales reps are spending less time on conversion work and more time on new customers, upsells, and selling more of the platform. We see this showing up in the Q1 results, where management said stronger new customer contribution was the main driver of the quarter’s outperformance. That is possibly one of the most important takeaways in the quarter. Recall that during the transition, one of the biggest drags was sales capacity, as they needed to allocate resources for migration vs. finding new customers. And that slowdown in new customer contribution may have led to some investors thinking demand has weakened. Now that the migration is done, the sales reps have more time to go after new customers, which should drive the next leg of growth.

There is another important takeaway here. Management said that long-term growth should lean more on the existing SaaS base, with the majority of annual contract value [ACV] expected to come from current customers. This is a huge statement because management is basically saying the actual value of the existing customer base is not reflected in the P&L today, and new customers acquired today will be worth a lot more in the coming years. And since we know more customers are going to be acquired (now that sales reps are focused on that), the customer lifetime value of the VRNS customer base should continue to see durable growth ahead.

AI is widening the platform

Now that the SaaS transition is done, the equity story now leans more on AI and data security. The basic idea is that customers are prioritizing data and AI security because guardrails are needed before they connect more of their data to AI systems. For those unaware, data security control is a real problem when it comes to adopting AI. Companies want the productivity benefit, but they do not want models and agents pulling sensitive data they should not touch.

This is where VRNS comes into play, and it is working out nicely. In this recent Q1, VRNS continued to see adoption of Managed Data Detection and Response [MDDR] and AI-related products, and also products that help to secure cloud environments. We also know that VRNS is winning large deals, as management pointed out a large new customer win from a global technology company with >50,000 employees, and that customer used VRNS across various environments (AWS, Salesforce, Google Cloud Platform, Google Drive, MDDR, Copilot, etc.). Even a large, prominent name like ServiceNow is also expanding its use of VRNS to cover internal AI systems and email security. This tells us that the VRNS platform is being used across multiple workloads and use cases. In simple terms, the product works and can work at scale.

This demand dynamic also changes the way I view VRNS’s newer product strategy. We know that customers are using VRNS, and if VRNS can roll out better products to address AI concerns, we should see healthy demand here. So, when management highlighted Database Activity Monitoring, Interceptor, and Atlas as seeing early traction, I thought that was very encouraging, as it suggests VRNS has a lever it can pull to accelerate growth.

Valuation

I remain bullish on VRNS. Looking at the growth momentum and potential, I raise my revenue growth assumptions, now expecting revenue growth to accelerate to 25% by FY2028, with FY2026 achieving the midpoint of management guidance (implying 18% y/y growth). Valuation-wise, the average revenue growth expectation is ~18% over the past 10 years, and the average forward revenue multiple over the same period is 7x. I am staying conservative here by assuming just 6x while growth is above the historical average. That brings me to a share price target of ~$61.

Risk

The growth narrative lies on better new customer momentum, broader platform adoption, and early interest in newer products like Atlas. If growth does not accelerate despite these, it may imply weak spots of demand somewhere in the business. This would weigh on the growth acceleration narrative that I expect to play out.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on VRNS. I believe the equity story is getting better. The SaaS transition is no longer an overhang; the cleanest growth metric accelerated; and demand is starting to broaden across more products and use cases. If topline growth accelerates as I expect, the stock should see a nice positive re-rate.

data.title: Constellium SE: Q1 Earnings Beat, Buy On Operational Growth
data.description:
Constellium SE (CSTM) is a global aluminum processing company that designs and manufactures high-value, engineered aluminum products that are used primarily in aerospace, automotive, and packaging applications. They don’t make their revenue from the raw aluminum itself, but rather from the value added by rolling, extruding, and engineering the metal into specialized components. The stock has seen tremendous growth in the last year, up 240%. The company just reported Q1 earnings with major wins and an optimistic outlook from management, with the stock up another 5% immediately on the news.

CSTM benefits from long-term structural growth in aluminum demand as manufacturers shift toward lighter and more energy-efficient materials. Earnings growth, cash flow guidance, and discounted valuation are green lights for this stock, and I have CSTM as a buy. The price of aluminum can cause them volatility, but their improvements in EBITDA per ton leave me less worried about the metal's pricing swings.

Q1 Snapshot

CSTM just reported a record adjusted EPS of $1.47 for Q1 2026, smoking expectations and up from $0.26 a year ago. That is now back-to-back quarters that earnings absolutely crushed analyst projections. Revenue was $2.5 billion, up 24% YoY, even though shipments were actually down 1% total.

Adjusted EBITDA has moved from $186 million last year in Q1 to $359 million this quarter, as each of their 3 segments saw nice advancements, with their largest segment, Packaging & Automotive Rolled Product, seeing a 152% increase. This gain does include a metal price lag positive impact of $97 million. Management alluded to favorable metal costs and lower operating costs as key drivers.

They repurchased 1.2 million shares for the quarter worth $28 million, bolstering investor confidence, and saw net income arrive at more than 400% higher than prior Q1. There is more to dissect, but the major fundamentals showcase a huge win for CSTM in Q1 results.

Guidance, Cash Flow & Leverage

CSTM CEO Ingrid Joerg noted that as a majorly global company, macroeconomic and geopolitical fronts have created some challenges, but the firm has remained persistent and even is raising guidance on the remainder of 2026. They now expect adjusted EBITDA in the range of $900-$940 million, not including metal lag effects, and also anticipate a huge ramp-up in free cash flow to reach more than $275 million. Free cash flow is rising on the price of aluminum rising significantly in the last year, as well as operational efficiencies improving the cash they make per ton sold. $275 million would be much higher than they have experienced in the past and would put them in a great position to weather any storm that comes with the cyclical nature of metal prices. The excess cash will also help in their deleveraging process that is now down to 2.2x, with hopes of reaching a target range as low as 1.5x.

Seeking Alpha analysts have EPS and revenue revisions all higher for CSTM, with a full-year earnings outlook of $2.09. I think this number is going to get smashed by year-end and will come in closer to $3.00, if not greater.

Aluminum Risk

No question the price return in the last year for CSTM can be at least partially correlated to the rapid rise in aluminum prices since about exactly a year ago. This is a risk for CSTM, as inevitably, aluminum prices will fall again. Personally, I don’t think that the metal is going to have a sharp drop anytime soon like it did in 2022 on recession fears and weakening demand. Revenue dropped for CSTM in 2023-2024 as the metal’s price was subdued, and this does need to be noted. However, there are two reasons that leave me less concerned about the volatility of the commodity for CSTM. Global aluminum demand looks quite strong for the next several years due to EV automotive, aircraft, and solar expansion. Number two, CSTM has done a fantastic job on enhancing operations, and has seen a generous improvement in cash from operations YoY and EBITDA per ton in each of their core segments.

Outpacing the Aluminum Risk

With aluminum prices fluctuating, it's important for CSTM to find another avenue toward sales or margin expansion. In many earnings calls, CSTM refers to “conversion margin,” essentially as their way of profiting from processing aluminum, not from owning the actual metal. There is not a specific ratio to measure this, but they do track EBITDA per ton shipped, which is the best available proxy to understand how efficient they are at producing and shipping aluminum.

CSTM saw adjusted EBITDA per ton increase by 6% in their Aerospace & Transportation segment, 54% in their Automotive Structure segment, and 159% in their Packing & Automotive Rolled Product segment. This is a fantastic sign that even when aluminum prices swing, they are positioning themselves to grow internally and not be dependent on the commodity itself.

The rising EBITDA per ton is telling us that they are seeing a better product mix with higher margin aluminum going to the aerospace and electric-vehicle industries in particular. These operational efficiency gains are demonstrating how the firm is moving more toward growing earnings per ton, not just tons shipped, and is trying to wean away from being just a commodity processor.

Solid Value

CSTM is trading at a discount from all angles. The stock has more than doubled in price since last fall and has managed to drop its PE from 87x to now 16x in that span. A remarkable run and position from a valuation standpoint. EPS over the last four quarters is $3.14, up from $0.46 in the prior 4 quarters. Their price-to-book is slightly elevated over the sector median, but price-to-sales and PEG are majorly discounted to the median. I think a PE closer to 30x is probably fair for CSTM. That would be a little above the industry, which I think is fair considering the growth they have had. I think the stock has very strong fundamentals and that investors have not yet fully priced in the company’s growth success.

Conclusion

CSTM just reported a huge beat in earnings along with strong guidance commentary from management in direction for the remainder of 2026. The stock is certainly reaping the benefits of the currently elevated price of aluminum. This is their biggest risk, but who’s to say that the metal isn’t going to remain high, considering demand across the globe is steady? Even with that risk, the company has done a fantastic job in improving on their “conversion margin,” with great advancement in their EBITDA per ton shipped. This is their ultimate pathway to growth in this industry and in becoming less reliant on metal pricing. Management expects their free cash flow to expand to record highs this year, and the stock is trading at a big discount at the moment. Buy CSTM.

data.title: TPG RE Finance Trust: Attractive Preferred Shares And A Robust Balance Sheet
data.description:
Thesis

As per their own statement, TPG Real Estate Finance Trust (TRTX) is a commercial real estate finance company sponsored by global alternative asset firm TPG. TRTX comes with a $650 million market capitalization and a track record exceeding ten years. In today's article we are going to focus on the latest results for TRTX and the impact on their preferred shares.

Company results and mandate

We have covered a number of CRE REITs, especially on the preferred equity side, and TRTX falls in that category. The company focuses on first mortgage loans, with a concentration on multi-family properties and industrial ones:

We can see a substantial decrease in the past year of the office loan portfolio, which migrated from 17.3% of the holdings to 10.6% now. Conversely, first mortgage loans on industrial properties increased from 4.9% to 19.5% in the past year. The main pillars of the REIT portfolio are therefore industrial and multifamily, with office loans being run down.

The office exposure has been further reduced since March 31, 2026, with the company stating the following on their April 2026 earnings call:

Regarding first quarter results, Doug Bouquard, Chief Executive Officer of TRTX, said: “During the first quarter of 2026, we out-earned our common stock dividend and maintained a 100% performing loan portfolio. Currently, our total office loan exposure has been reduced to less than 5%, and nearly 70% of our loan portfolio consists of newer vintage originations made after January 2023 secured primarily by multifamily and industrial collateral. Our loan portfolio and well-capitalized balance sheet will enable us to take advantage of our robust investment pipeline through the remainder of 2026.”

It is very important to note the vintage of the portfolio, because most of the problems for CRE REITs come from loans originated before 2023, when rates were low and property values were high. A loan portfolio mainly centered after the collapse in property values ensures a much healthier recovery and low probability of default (i.e. business plans and net incomes were projected with the new reality in mind).

In fact, the company's credit reserves have been extremely stable in the past year, with no reserves taken in March 2026:

The company increased its credit reserves only in December 2025 by $4 M, and has not added anything in the past quarter. The portfolio risk rating has also remained very stable:

If we look at the internal weighted average risk rating, we will notice it remained at 3 for the quarter, unchanged from year-end 2025. A higher risk rating (with a maximum of 5) means a higher probability of default (i.e. a bad outcome). The lower the risk rating, the better. These are very good results and generally expected given the vintage of the portfolio.

Expanding lending and leverage

The REIT is looking to boost earnings via doing more lending:

The REIT has continued to increase its lending portfolio, which now stands at $4.144 billion, an increase from the last quarter. This is how a healthy company behaves when faced with the bottom of a cycle - it lends in order to take advantage of wide spreads and good LTVs.

The increase in the balance sheet has come at the expense of leverage, with a higher gearing ratio:

Leverage is only bad when headed into a recession or when new business is done at the peak of the cycle (think 2021). It is not the case here. The company has demonstrated its ability to pick good credits; it has shifted its portfolio from the problematic office space and into more stable segments, thus an increase in leverage is not an issue.

The company's strategy makes sense when looking at how it has managed to stabilize its book value in the past year after the massive shock caused by the 2022 rate increases:

Book value has stabilized above $13/share in the past year, with the potential for an increase if accretive net income is generated by an increase in leverage.

We can see the market also reflecting the company's outlook via a stabilization of the price-to-book ratio:

We are of the opinion that once the company goes above 90% of newer vintage loans and provides clear guidance on its REO assets, we can see the market price go back towards a flat price-to-book.

Coverage metrics

The company has only one series of preferred shares outstanding, namely the Series C (TRTX.PR.C). The interest coverage is above 4.8x when adjusting for management fees and compensation expenses:

Coverage metrics are very good, and they are to be seen from the lens of a company that is increasing its projected net income figure.

Preferred shares analytics

Let us now look at some of the analytics associated with the preferred shares:

  • Current price: $18/share
  • Current yield: 8.7%
  • Perpetual: yes
  • Cumulative: yes
  • Call date: 9/15/2026
  • Coupon: 5.75% fixed

The shares have a very low cost of capital for the company, with a coupon of only 5.75%; thus, investors should think of them as a permanent form of capital. We do not anticipate them to ever be called unless we go back to a zero-rate environment (very unlikely).

We have established in the analysis above that the company's balance sheet is strong and improving, that its vintage holdings are good, and that the increase in leverage in today's environment can be NAV accretive. This positions the preferred shares to pertain to a robust, healthy CRE REIT. Let us remind ourselves what a bad story for preferred shares in the CRE REIT land looks like:

This is the Series E from Ready Capital (RC) trading at current yields in excess of 13% and with a price point in the low teens.

We have covered a few CRE REIT preferred shares, and the healthy ones are trading in an 8.5% to 9.5% current yield range:

  • KREF.PR.A: 9% current yield
  • FBRT.PR.E: 9.5% current yield
  • TRTX.PR.C: 8.7% current yield

The TPG RE Finance preferred shares are trading towards the bottom of the range given the robust balance sheet backing them. Below is a quick matrix on various prices and current yields:

We like TRTX.PR.C is here and is of the opinion that TRTX's balance sheet is net superior to KREF's. The spread differential is very small, thus making TRTX.PR.C is very attractive for us.

Conclusion

TPG Real Estate Finance is a CRE REIT backed by TPG. The fund has stabilized its book value and has moved its book of business towards industrial and multifamily loans. The latest results show no increase in the portfolio risk rating and no new credit reserves. The name is moving towards increasing lending in order to grow its net income. The preferred shares have ample interest coverage and come with a long duration and a high current yield of 8.7%. We like TRTX's balance sheet and strategic direction and find the TRTX.PR.C yields appeal in the current macro.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Mid Cap Value Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The leadership rotation and broadening of the market that began in October continued in early 2026. Investors were encouraged by a positive ISM Manufacturing PMI® reading and other signs of economic strengthening. Simultaneously, artificial intelligence (AI) advancements intensified fears that the technology will disrupt many businesses, particularly those offering products or services that could be delivered more effectively by AI agents. This led many investors to favor shares of companies with heavy assets and low obsolescence risk, a group that market commentators began referring to as “HALO stocks.” The small- and mid-cap value segments of the market benefited disproportionately from investor interest in this cohort.

However, in the final weeks of the quarter, confidence in continued economic strengthening began to erode after the start of Operation Epic Fury, a joint assault on Iran by the US and Israel. Iran responded aggressively to the attacks and effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a critically important shipping lane for oil, natural gas, and petrochemicals produced in the region. As prices for these commodities surged, shares of many energy and materials companies rallied, while most others, particularly the recently favored HALO stocks, declined as investors considered the possibility of a prolonged military conflict that would increase costs for the global economy and negatively impact corporate profits.

Although small- and mid-cap value stocks retreated from their February highs after the commencement of Operation Epic Fury, they still outpaced other market segments by a wide margin in the first quarter. The Russell Midcap Value® Index, the Carillon Chartwell Mid Cap Value Fund’s benchmark, generated a total return of 3.7%. The best-performing sectors in the index were energy, information technology, and materials.

The worst-performing sectors were financials, communication services and healthcare.

Fund Review

Top securities

Littelfuse (LFUS) designs and manufactures fuses, power semiconductors, electronic equipment, and circuit protection components. Investors reacted favorably to an accelerating growth outlook and margin leverage, driven by expanding data center design wins and improving momentum across electronics and industrial end markets.

Regal Rexnord (RRX) manufactures motion control components, power transmission solutions, and specialty electric motors and controls. The company reported improving industrial demand trends and highlighted accelerating momentum with data center customers.

Baker Hughes (BKR) is a global industrial technology and energy services provider, offering equipment and solutions for industrial, energy transition, and oil and gas applications. Shares were supported by strong growth and order momentum in the industrial and energy technology segment, driven by power systems and data center demand.

Keysight Technologies (KEYS) supplies electronic test and measurement solutions used across communications, aerospace and defense, semiconductor, and industrial markets. The company reported broad-based growth and raised its full-year outlook, driven by accelerating AI demand and strengthening conditions across industrial end markets.

Kirby (KEX) transports liquid cargo on inland and coastal waterways and provides engine services, power systems, and oilfield equipment. Shares benefited as investors gained confidence in sustained marine utilization and pricing, supported by a stronger chemical backdrop and continued momentum in the company's power generation business.

Bottom securities

Bruker (BRKR) is a global leader in scientific instruments and analytical solutions for the life sciences, pharmaceutical, and industrial research markets. The company reported weaker-than-expected margin performance and guidance, as continued softness in academic and government end markets weighed on confidence in the pace of the earnings recovery.

Avantor (AVTR) provides chemicals, reagents, and laboratory supplies to customers in the biopharmaceutical, healthcare, and government sectors. Shares were weak amid ongoing revenue and margin pressure and waning confidence in the company's turnaround efforts.

Synchrony Financial (SYF) is a consumer finance company specializing in private label and co-branded credit card programs. Shares lagged amid regulatory uncertainty around potential credit card interest-rate caps and ongoing pressure on its low- and middle-income borrowers.

Outlook

As the second quarter gets underway, investors are intensely focused on the war in the Middle East and its global economic impact. Currently, our base case assumption is that economic considerations and domestic political realities will compel the Trump administration to pursue a negotiated ceasefire with Iran and a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. If this occurs in the next few weeks, we believe damage to the global economy could be contained and that growth could reaccelerate. But this or any other potential outcome is highly uncertain, and we will continually retest our assumptions as the situation evolves.

Previous periods of macroeconomic-driven market volatility have created opportunities for us to upgrade the Fund's portfolio, and we believe this latest episode has been no different. Our disciplined focus on value, quality, and the potential for fundamental improvement has continued to drive stock selection. The positioning of the Fund reflects this bottom-up approach, as well as our commitment to maintaining balanced sector and industry exposures, which is of even greater importance when the economic outlook is this murky. At the end of the first quarter, the Fund was modestly overweight to the industrials, healthcare, and consumer discretionary sectors and modestly underweight to the financials, communication services, and information technology sectors.

data.title: 5 Dividend Increases Expected In May 2026
data.description:
We are always on the lookout for dividend increases. Whether it is on our website or social media platforms, we are always sharing dividend increase news. In this article, we are featuring 5 expected dividend increases in May 2026. You won't want to miss it!

Why do we suspect these companies are going to announce a dividend increase in May? The dividend stocks featured in this article all have a history of announcing their annual dividend increases in May. The funny thing about large, blue-chip, dividend-paying stocks is that they are generally pretty consistent with dividend announcements. Management teams prefer announcing and paying dividends around the same time every quarter. Plus, shareholders like us also enjoy the predictability and consistency of dividend payments. Especially once you retire and live on your dividend income.

The same consistency applies for dividend increase announcements. For the most part, companies announce their annual dividend increases in the same month every year. (Of course, there are always exceptions to the rule.) Each of the featured dividend stocks, when reviewing their history, has announced dividend increases in May in recent history. That is why we are so confident that each of the featured dividend stocks will once again increase its dividend in May.

Why Dividend Increases Matter

Dividends are the foundation of our goal to reach financial freedom. We continuously invest in dividend growth stocks to grow our passive income. If you haven't figured it out by now, we eat, sleep, and breathe dividends. With high inflation, buying undervalued dividend stocks that increase your dividend is as crucial as ever.

That is why dividend growth is a major deciding factor in all of our stock purchase decisions. It is the third metric of our Dividend Stock Screener for a reason. For each stock, we review a company's history of increasing dividends (consecutive annual dividend increases) and its five-year average dividend growth rate.

data.title: On Holding: A Strong Business, But A Mispriced Stock
data.description:
Why The Recent Selloff Deserves a Closer Look

On Holding (ONON) fell roughly 27% in the last year, even with the business delivering strong growth. The Swiss athletic footwear company was founded in 2010 and became known mostly because of its CloudTec cushioning technology. The company started in running but has expanded into apparel and tennis (On partnered with Roger Federer, launching his own collection) and training and currently sells in more than 90 countries, with a strong DTC presence.

On's stock price is currently trading at $35.07, far from its 52-week high of $61.29, and there are a couple of reasons for that. The selloffs were driven by FY2025 results, which showed a GAAP net income below expectation, mostly because of FX (the company reports in Swiss francs), and the announcement that the CEO, after a five-year tenure (+13 years as CFO), will step down in May.

In my opinion, there was an overreaction, and this is an excellent opportunity for a buy. Martin Hoffmann (CEO leaving on May 1) will stay as an advisor until March 2027, and the co-founders David Allemann and Caspar Coppetti will step up as co-CEOs. Also, the biggest market for On is in the U.S., responsible for more than 50% of its revenue, so the FX matter is more an accounting problem than a profitability one.

On Is Still Growing Well

The FY2025 results triggered a reaction in the stock price, but it's actually pretty solid for me. Revenue hit CHF 3B, crossing for the first time the CHF 3B mark, up 30% YoY (35.6% on a constant-currency basis). Gross margin was 62.8% in 2025, up 2.2% compared to last year, meaning that the company is getting more profitable. Adjusted EBITDA was CHF 567M, while margin was 18.8%. All of these were records, so why did the stock price fall?

I mentioned before that the company reports in Swiss francs, while more than half of its revenue comes from the U.S. In 2025, GAAP net income fell 15.9%, which justifies the selloff, right? Not really. The company had an unrealized loss of CHF 173M, representing pretty much the whole decrease in net income the company had. On didn't lose money; it's merely an accounting loss, and it doesn't represent a problem in the operational performance.

To clarify, the next numbers I mentioned here will be in a constant currency. On has been expanding its business, with revenue generated in Asia-Pacific roughly doubling and passing the CHF 500M, representing 17% of sales. Europe-Middle East-Africa is up 34.7%, while the Americas increased 23.4%. 42% of sales now comes from DTC, which is great in my opinion, because the company can have even better margins by reducing costs.

Going through the products, the footwear business is still the main one responsible for sales, which are up roughly 33%. But the company is investing in apparel (up 76%) and accessories (up 135%), and they currently represent 7% of sales. I see this diversification as very important to consolidate the brand but also to have different sources of revenue in the long term.

Besides that, On has CHF 1B in cash and almost no financial debt. The company grew through equity, and this amount of cash in a company with a 0.036 debt-to-equity ratio (0.35 if we take into consideration the store leases) allows the company to expand aggressively.

Why The Current Valuation Looks Attractive

On is currently trading at 57% of its 52-week high and close to prices the company traded at in mid-2024. EV/forward EBITDA is at 13x, lower than last year's 30x and than peers like Nike, but On has an estimated growth of 23% in 2026. The company is growing, becoming more profitable, and has very low debt, but still, the price keeps going down.

I believe that, buying the stock now, you are getting a much better business than it was in 2024 for the same price. EV/Forward Sales is at 3x, but peers like Deckers is at 2.8x, but Deckers is guiding for 9% growth in 2026 and 7% in 2027, while On is aiming for 23%, like I said.

I built a scenario analysis where I used Seeking Alpha's EPS estimates of $1.66 for 2026 and $2.07 for 2027.

As a bear case, I used a ratio of 18x, which is the level mature companies close to what Adidas, Deckers, and Lululemon trade at currently. For my base case, I used 27x, which is a 50% premium over mature companies and is defensible considering that On traded over the last two years with an average premium of 57% over the comparable peers, and at the same time it's conservative compared to the historical average multiple the company has traded. For my bull case, I used 33x, which is close to the average multiple the stock sustained before the selloff in May 2024. If growth continues, I don't see a reason why the company should be trading at lower multiples than 2024.

So the bear case gave me prices that, in 2027, will be close to what the stock is trading at right now. The base case gave an implied return of 58% by the end of 2027, while the bull case (which is the most realistic one for me, but I want to be conservative here) gave an implied return of roughly 90% by 2027. I see a clear asymmetry here, with risk/reward pointing to the upside.

Another way to value the stock is to compare ratios like EV/Revenue, EV/EBITDA, and P/BV to its peers. ON has historically traded at a premium, and using this premium (two-year historical average) to value the company by the end of 2026, we would get an average price in the high $40s, which is in between the base and bull case for P/E. To calculate that, I used estimated revenue, EBITDA, and BVPS from FactSet for 2026. For all of these reasons, I see On as a buy.

What Could Unlock Upside

I think that the Q1'26 earnings report (date still TBD) and management presentation should give us some important answers. The quarter will have been under Martin Hoffmann as CEO, but it will be important to see how the new management conducts the earnings call. This could help close the gap between the fundamentals and the reaction the market had when the CEO change was announced.

Also, we will see how the company is doing in China and APAC. I believe that meaningful growth in this region could trigger an upside unlock in price, and with companies like Nike (NYSE:NKE) losing market share in this area, I think that expressive growth would not be a surprise.

I believe that LightSpray technology could be another catalyst, taking On's production to another level. The company announced a new manufacturing facility in South Korea that can reduce the production process to a single automated step of 3 minutes, performed by a robot with minimal material waste. I think that, If On can scale this technology across more products, margins would grow, and the premium On trades right now could be even higher.

What Could Pressure The Thesis

I see the tariffs as the most important short-term risk for the business. 90% of footwear and 65% of apparel come from Vietnam, and this exposure, if the U.S. Government gets more aggressive on tariffs, could hurt the margins. By now, management has been able to partially mitigate this problem, but the U.S. trade policy remains a risk.

The CEO transition is also a risk that should be taken into consideration. The market already reacted to the news, and I get that Martin Hoffmann was the face of the business, but the co-founders have been involved in the business since day one. I believe that the underpromising and overdelivering culture will keep going and the market overreacted. Allemann has a background in design, and Coppetti in strategy, and they have been in charge before.

Also, the athletic footwear industry is one of the most competitive, with huge companies like Nike and Adidas, but new companies are growing like Hoka, Anta, and Li Ning (not to mention Under Armour, Puma, Deckers...). This competitive environment is a risk by itself, but for a company like On, which sells at full price and has margins close to luxury brands, sales could be hurt in a weaker macro environment.

Why I Still Rate On A Buy

For me, On looks even more attractive after the selloffs. The stock price fell for reasons that don't really have to do with the business model itself, like the effect of FX on net income and the CEO transition. At this price, I see an asymmetry in the risk/reward tradeoff very favorable to investors, with the stock trading close to what it was in May 2024, even though the business is larger and more profitable. I see the bear case already priced in, and the valuation scenarios point to a fair price in the high $40s, and for these reasons I think On Holding is a buy.

data.title: Amazon Q1: Anthropic Investment Is Just The Tip Of The AI Iceberg
data.description:
I believe a good way to start this article is by saying that Amazon (AMZN) is the company with the largest revenue in the world. It is $742.7 billion in revenue over the last 12 months. I am saying this because Amazon delivered a YoY growth of 17% in Q1.

To give you an idea, Walmart’s top line (WMT), which is in second place, grows in the mid-single digits, something close to 4%-5%.

This alone is already a taste of how strong Amazon’s momentum is, but Q1 earnings are full of other good indicators that make me even more optimistic about Amazon’s long-term case.

Why I Liked AMZN’s Q1 Earnings

Amazon’s Q1 earnings seem really solid looking at the double (or triple) beat. Reported revenue was ~$4.3 billion above what the market expected, EPS was $1 above expectations (including non-recurring effects), and even more interesting, Q2 top-line guidance was $7.6 billion above. These are very strong numbers, especially if we stop to think that the low end of the Q2 revenue range is already higher than what consensus expected.

This is positive, of course. But that is not what made me more optimistic about Amazon. In fact, I got more excited about the quarter when I saw this table:

I believe that just this information is enough to illustrate why Amazon’s momentum is so strong. At first glance, International net sales grew 11%, and North America grew 12%. Positive numbers, right? Yes, but the breakdown is even better. Online stores had solid momentum, high single-digit growth. Physical stores had weaker momentum than other quarters.

But note how third-party seller services, which is already a very robust revenue stream, grew 12%, above previous quarters. Also notice how advertising services maintained robust growth of 22% YoY even while already being at a more mature level. Also note that subscription services delivered a 12% growth.

All these revenues have more interesting margins than traditional e-commerce and show how the revenue mix is improving and how Amazon's ecosystem is becoming more robust. The impact of this is very noticeable in the operating margin. Even growing more than in previous quarters, North America's operating margin was 7.9%. To give an idea, in Q4 in 2024 (excellent tailwind due to seasonality), the margin was 8%. What I mean here is that in just over a year, Amazon made the quarter with one of the seasonally weakest margins as solid as the best quarter from a few years ago. This is also true for the international segment, which reached a 3.6% margin, better than the 3% of Q1 2025.

But AWS, which is one of the great pillars of the Amazon case, also presented very good results. Note how the 28% revenue growth is much better than previous quarters. In fact, Andy Jassy mentioned that this was the fastest growth in 15 quarters.

As I just analyzed Alphabet’s (GOOGL) (GOOG) results (check it here), I am a bit biased: Google’s cloud division just delivered 60%+ YoY growth. That makes Amazon’s 28% not look like much. But it is important to remember that while Google Cloud revenue is ~$20 billion per quarter, AWS just reached $37.5 billion. It is already at a much more mature level.

And even at this level of revenue, the prospects for the cloud division are very good. The backlog is $364 billion (and according to management, it is not “just 1 customer or 2 customers”). This is an essential point to counter the bear thesis on free cash flow.

Looking at the chart below without context, you might think Amazon is in a fragile momentum. But the context is that Amazon is generating massive cash flow while also making massive CapEx, precisely to meet this demand (backlog) and invest in the business itself. That is, if it is spending ~$150 billion in CapEx annually, but in return is growing its Cloud business very fast, a revenue stream that Amazon should be able to keep collecting for a long time, and with healthy margins (as it seems), the ROIC should be positive, not to mention internal margin gains from automation, robotics, etc.

Amazon Valuation: It Makes Sense

Still on free cash flow, it is the reason why the valuation makes sense.

Amazon is a ~$2.8 trillion company. Free cash flow over the last 12 months was $1.2 billion. I will not even calculate the P/FCF, but it is already clear that it would be a ridiculous multiple and would make Amazon look like a meme stock. But look at the first line. Amazon had $148.5 billion in operating cash flow over the last 12 months.

So for valuation we need to consider two factors. The first is what happens to shareholder value when Amazon decides it already has a “large enough” infrastructure and brings CapEx back to ~$70 billion. Well, that would leave about $80 billion in free cash flow.

Still not the best multiple, right? It is close to 35x. But we have to remember that $70 billion is already a large number. It would not be just maintenance CapEx (which should increase as infrastructure grows, depreciation will continue to increase, etc.). That is, even after drastically reducing CapEx, $70 billion would still represent a growth stage. In addition, investments will continue to mature, the revenue mix should keep improving, and all this infrastructure and new acquisitions also allow tons of optionalities to eventually appear in the financials, as I mentioned in my last article (check it here).

I also need to mention again the chip business that had further progress this quarter, with OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) signing an agreement to use Trainium capacity/chips. Speaking of Anthropic, this was one of the non-recurring items that boosted EPS: $16.8 billion of “non-operating income from investments in Anthropic”.

Projecting this, multiples become more attractive. Thinking in terms of EV-to-EBIT, it is possible to reach 16.4x for 2030 with assumptions that I consider conservative - assuming all revenues gradually decelerate in growth pace until 2030 but with gradual EBIT margin expansion.

In this scenario, I believe free cash flow could be even higher, since depreciation does not enter cash flow, which makes the ~6% shareholder yield (proxy) for 2030 even more feasible.

Bottom Line

Even at current prices, Amazon still seems to have some margin of safety, mainly due to strong potential in the coming years. The long term seems very promising, both in top line growth through the core and new ventures and in margin expansion. A good mental exercise is to think about what Amazon will look like in 2040. How strong will its robotics division be (which is already strong today)? How strong could other segments not on any analyst’s radar be (like Zoox, which is a self-driving car initiative)? Consequently, how much can revenue continue to grow (compound) until then, and how much margin can it reach? All of this can have an explosive effect on shareholder value when we are talking about decades.

As I mentioned in my last article, this is also one of the “risks” for the case: Amazon is such a large company that it ends up facing very strict regulation, and the better and larger the company becomes, the more uncertain regulatory issues become, such as how much governments in different regions may want to regulate, etc.

In this context, I maintain my buy rating for AMZN post Q1 earnings.

data.title: Energizer Holdings Is Getting Harder To Resist
data.description:
Back in September of last year, I wrote a bullish article about Energizer Holdings (ENR). In that article, I acknowledged that the company was underperforming compared to the market. However, it was showing certain promising financial trends. Since then, however, the stock has fallen 31.4%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 6.8%. This return disparity is discouraging, to say the least. And it comes at a time when management expects the 2026 fiscal year to be worse than 2025 was. The good news is that, even though this year might be worse, the stock is still incredibly cheap. Furthermore, there are certain trends that should turn around that will prove to be a positive for the company. That's why I am not worried about the ‘buy’ rating I assigned it back then.

This doesn't mean that my opinion cannot or will not change. If new data comes in that justifies the change, then I will change my opinion. The good news here is that we won't have to wait too long before new data is incorporated into the picture. I say this because, before the market opens on May 5th, management will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue is expected to dip slightly. And profitability is forecasted to follow suit. But unless something really big and unexpected comes out that hurts the valuation of the company, I don't think I will change my mind.

I’m still energized about Energizer Holdings

Before we get into financial performance expectations for the upcoming quarterly release, I think it would be helpful to dig into the most recent data that investors have. This involves the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $778.9 million. That was actually up nicely from the $731.7 million that the business reported a year earlier. In the chart below, you can see that the company only saw an increase in revenue thanks to acquisition activities. In May of last year, the company completed its purchase of Advanced Power Solutions. This was in exchange for $16.7 million. Even though that was a relatively small purchase, it was responsible for $64.6 million of revenue in the first quarter of 2026. The company also saw a $13.7 million benefit from foreign currency fluctuations. But, based on the data provided, organic revenue dropped by $31.2 million.

Even though revenue increased, profitability took a hit. The firm went from a net profit of $22.3 million to a net loss of $3.4 million. Even on an adjusted basis, we get a decline in profitability from $49.4 million to $21.3 million. This is not to say that every metric worsened. Operating cash flow skyrocketed from $77 million to $149.5 million. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $65.3 million to $74.6 million. On the other hand, EBITDA for the business contracted from $140.7 million to $106.9 million.

When it comes to the specific operating segments of the business, we end up getting an interesting picture. In the chart above, you can see financial performance associated with its Batteries & Lights segment. Revenue jumped from $632.9 million to $685.2 million year over year. All of this was because of the aforementioned acquisition, as well as a $12.3 million benefit coming from foreign currency fluctuations and a slight increase associated with activities in highly inflationary markets that it operates in. But organic revenue dropped $24.3 million for the quarter. Management blamed this on lower volumes caused by softer consumer demand in the US in both October and November. It is also worth noting, however, that the picture is a bit distorted by the fact that there was increased buying activity a year earlier because of increased storm damage. I wish I could say that profitability followed revenue higher. But that was not the case. Because of the pain associated with the organic revenue decline, segment profits fell from $119.3 million to $105.7 million. Management said that some of this impact came from tariffs, in addition to temporary costs that the company incurred as it transitioned its network in order to address that headwind.

We also have the Auto Care segment. Year over year, revenue dropped from $99.3 million to $93.7 million. All of this, or at least most of it, was the result of a $6.9 million hit to organic revenue. According to management, this pain was the result of volume declines caused by consumer softness in the US and the fact that the company had experienced strong performance in international markets the previous year. A change in product mix also affected the company in a negative way. Sadly, as revenue contracted, profitability fell also, dropping from $20.5 million to $9.1 million. The organic revenue decline did the most damage, with management specifically pointing out direct impacts from tariffs and the aforementioned transition aimed at combating them.

Unfortunately, this year is likely to be a bit of a problem for the company. Management mentioned that EBITDA, for starters, should come in at between $580 million and $610 million for the year. That compares to the $623.6 million the business reported for 2025. Adjusted earnings per share should range between $3.30 and $3.60. Hitting the midpoint would give us adjusted net profits of $236 million compared to the $253.1 million that the business reported a year earlier. And if we assume that adjusted operating cash flow will scale down similarly, we would get a reading for this year of $267.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see what all of this means for the company from a valuation standpoint. The stock is, quite honestly, very cheap. In fact, it is so cheap that I find it difficult to not upgrade it to a ‘strong buy’. Relative to other similar enterprises, the stock is cheap as well. The aforementioned chart shows that it is cheaper than any of the five businesses I stacked it up against.

Hopefully, in the coming days, investors will get more insight from management regarding the health of the business. As I mentioned already, analysts are expecting revenue to fall. They believe that sales will come in for the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year at $661.9 million. That would be down slightly from the $662.9 million that the business reported for the second quarter of 2025. Earnings per share have actually not been estimated by analysts. But adjusted earnings per share should come in at around $0.47. That compares to the $0.67 reported a year earlier and would translate to a drop in profitability from $49.4 million to $32.1 million. The table below shows other profitability metrics for the second quarter of 2025. It's not unthinkable that the firm could experience weakness when it comes to most or all of these as well.

One thing that certainly brings me comfort is the thought that the company should, in the long run, do just fine. In the image below, you can see that the business did end up seeing some strength heading into the end of 2025. This data shows a rolling four-week window of time that shows a real recovery starting around the middle of November. So that is promising in and of itself. But there are other reasons to be optimistic also.

Management continues to invest in different opportunities. One example of this would be the specialty battery space. Overall sales in that market have jumped 25% over the last four years. And the company currently has the number one market share position in this category. Increased power from newer devices is causing average change frequency to increase, which should bode well for the business. And in addition to this, the company has made other innovations, such as rolling out the first-ever 3-in-1 Child Shield technology.

We also have certain cost-cutting efforts. In the first quarter of 2026, for instance, the company achieved certain savings, including $20 million in annual tariff mitigation costs and another $20 million in non-tariff-related expenditures. From the first quarter of 2026 through the final quarter of the year, the company also expects to benefit from between $15 million and $20 million of incremental production credits. Plus, the company is actively focused on paying down debt. In fact, during the first quarter of the year, it paid down around $100 million of debt while simultaneously spending $5 million on share buybacks and $23 million on dividends. In an ideal world, the company would flip the spending between share repurchases and dividends. But the fact that management has been able to keep up those activities while reducing leverage is great.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I really struggled with whether to upgrade Energizer Holdings to a ‘strong buy’ or not. I like a lot of what I am seeing, but I do want to see some additional stabilization before I turn bullish on the business. For now, that means I should stick with my ‘buy’ rating. But it really would not take me much to upgrade it further.
